title: Acts
author: Luke, physician and companion of Paul (continuation of the Gospel of Luke)
setting: Addressed to Theophilus (Luke 1:3; Acts 1:1), Acts records the expansion of the church from Jerusalem to Rome — fulfilling Jesus' commission in Acts 1:8.
purpose: To show that the gospel of Jesus Christ is the power of God advancing unstoppably from Jerusalem, through Judea and Samaria, to the ends of the earth — carried by the Spirit-empowered witness of the apostles despite opposition, imprisonment, and persecution.
themes: The Holy Spirit as the empowering agent of mission
The unstoppable advance of the gospel
The inclusion of Gentiles in the covenant people
The witness of the apostles under trial
The lordship of the risen Christ

outline.label: Jerusalem: The Church's Birth (Pentecost to Stephen)
Judea and Samaria: The Church Scattered
Paul's Second and Third Missionary Journeys
Paul's Arrest, Trials, and Journey to Rome

before.label: Joel 2:28–32 — Spirit poured on all flesh; the 'last days' begin
Resurrection and 40-day ministry of the risen Christ
Jesus' Great Commission — 'to the ends of the earth'

after.label: Paul's letters written from within the Acts narrative
Paul arrives in Rome — the gospel reaches the empire's capital
Persecution scatters and refines — witnesses sealed by their testimony as the apostles foresaw

key_people.name: Peter
key_people.role: The dominant figure of the first half (chs. 1-12) who preaches at Pentecost, heals the lame man, defends the gospel before the Sanhedrin, and opens the door to Gentiles at Cornelius's house

key_people.name: Paul
key_people.role: The dominant figure of the second half (chs. 13-28) whose three missionary journeys and journey to Rome trace the gospel's movement from Jerusalem to the centre of the world

key_people.name: Stephen
key_people.role: The first martyr whose speech (ch. 7) is the longest in Acts and a comprehensive reinterpretation of Israel's history culminating in the accusation that the religious leaders have killed the Righteous One

key_people.name: Barnabas
key_people.role: Paul's missionary partner whose role as bridge-builder (between Paul and the Jerusalem church; between the gospel and Gentile culture) is crucial for the mission's success

key_people.name: Lydia, Cornelius, the Philippian Jailer
key_people.role: Representative converts from different social locations whose stories illustrate the gospel's power across the boundaries of race, class, and religion

context: Acts was written by Luke c. AD 80-90 as the second volume of his two-volume work. It traces the expansion of the gospel from Jerusalem (chs. 1-7), through Judaea and Samaria (chs. 8-12), to the ends of the earth as represented by Rome (chs. 13-28). The narrative is shaped by the promise of Acts 1:8 and driven by the Holy Spirit, who directs, empowers, and advances the mission at every decisive moment.
christ_connection: Acts is the story of the exalted and reigning Christ advancing his kingdom through the Spirit-empowered witness of his church. Every sermon in Acts centres on the resurrection of Jesus as the eschatological event that inaugurates the age of the Spirit (Joel 2:28-32 applied to Pentecost), establishes Jesus as Lord and Christ (2:36), and demands repentance and faith. The theological claim is that the Jesus of Nazareth who was crucified is the risen Lord who now sits at God's right hand (2:33-34) and from that position continues to act through his people in the world.

key_verses.note: 'You will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth' — the book's structure in a sentence
'Let all the house of Israel therefore know for certain that God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified' — the kerygmatic core: the risen and exalted Jesus is Lord
'And there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved' — the book's exclusive claim in its most concentrated form

themes_detail.title: The Holy Spirit as the Book's Primary Agent
themes_detail.text: The Spirit is mentioned over 50 times in Acts and is the driving force behind every major development in the narrative: the Pentecost outpouring (2:1-4), the bold preaching of Peter (4:8), the direction of Philip (8:29, 39), the call of Paul and Barnabas (13:2), the Jerusalem Council's decision (15:28), and Paul's route changes (16:6-7). The Spirit is not a vague divine force but acts with specificity, speaks, forbids, and sends. Luke's narrative argues that what is happening in the church's expansion is not human religious achievement but God's own work through the Spirit who was poured out at Pentecost.

themes_detail.title: The Gospel Crossing Boundaries
themes_detail.text: Acts is structured as a series of boundary-crossings that expand the definition of who belongs to God's people: from Jewish pilgrims (2:5-11) to Samaritans (ch. 8), to a God-fearing Ethiopian (8:26-40), to a Roman centurion (ch. 10-11), to Gentiles throughout the Mediterranean world (chs. 13-28). Each crossing is preceded by divine direction and confirmed by the Spirit's outpouring. The Jerusalem Council (ch. 15) makes the theological argument explicit: Gentiles receive the same Spirit as Jewish believers, demonstrating that God has accepted them. The gospel is, from the beginning, for all nations.

themes_detail.title: The Continuity of the Mission with Israel's Story
themes_detail.text: Every major speech in Acts begins with Israel's history and argues that what is happening in Jesus and his community is the continuation and fulfilment of that story. Stephen's speech (ch. 7) retells the entire OT narrative to show that Israel had always resisted God's chosen deliverers; Paul's speech at Pisidian Antioch (13:16-41) moves from the Exodus to David to Jesus; and his speech at Athens (17:22-31) begins with creation. Acts presents Christianity not as a new religion but as the Jewish scriptures' own fulfilment — the covenant God at last fully enacting his saving purposes.

themes_detail.title: Suffering and the Advance of the Gospel
themes_detail.text: Acts' structure suggests a counterintuitive pattern: persecution advances rather than hinders the gospel. The scattering after Stephen's martyrdom takes the gospel to Samaria and beyond (8:1-4); Paul's imprisonment leads to the Philippian jailer's conversion (16:25-34); his appeal to Caesar takes the gospel to Rome (27-28). The theology behind this pattern is explicit in Paul's speech to the Ephesian elders (20:24): the ministry must be completed regardless of what it costs, because it was received from the Lord Jesus. Suffering is not an obstacle to the mission but one of its primary vehicles.
